excerpt_json: The library lights were too bright for how late it was, but she stayed anyway. Her notes were scattered, her thoughts even more so.
Everyone else looked focused, like they had a plan. She wasn’t sure she even understood the question.
Still, she kept typing. Even if it was messy. Even if it wasn’t perfect.
Because stopping felt worse than trying.
The train platform stretched out beneath the dim overhead lights. A few people stood apart, each waiting in their own space.
The sound of distant tracks echoed faintly, growing louder with time.
A breeze moved through the station, shifting coats and loose papers.
Then the train arrived, and everything moved forward again.
She stared at the door longer than she meant to. Inside, someone was already asking questions—confident, clear, everything she wasn’t.
What if hers didn’t make sense? What if she was the only one struggling?
But she remembered how lost she felt yesterday. And how much even a small answer helped.
So she knocked, even if her voice shook.
The café was filled with quiet conversation and the soft clink of cups.
People came and went, each lingering just long enough before moving on.
Sunlight filtered through the windows, shifting slowly across the tables.
Time passed without urgency.
They all seemed to understand the assignment immediately. She nodded along, hoping no one would notice the gap between them and her.
When it was her turn to speak, she hesitated just a second too long.
But then she tried anyway—piecing together what she could.
And someone nodded back, like it made sense.
The path curved through the quiet park, lined with trees that barely moved in the still air.
A few people passed by, their footsteps soft against the pavement.
The sky shifted slowly toward dusk, colors fading into gray.
Everything felt steady, unchanged.
She walked out of the exam room replaying every answer in her head.
Everyone else seemed relieved, already talking about what they got right.
She wasn’t sure she got anything right.
But she had shown up. She had tried. And maybe that counted for something.
The shelves were lined with books, each one carefully arranged in its place.
Dust floated in the air, visible only when the light hit it just right.
A student passed by, scanning titles without stopping.
The room remained quiet and unchanged.
She chose a seat near the middle—close enough to see, far enough to disappear.
People around her chatted easily, already forming connections.
She wondered if she should say something, or if it was already too late.
But when someone turned and smiled, it felt like maybe it wasn’t.
Rain tapped steadily against the window, forming small trails down the glass.
The room inside remained quiet, insulated from the sound.
Outside, the world moved more slowly, blurred by the falling rain.
Nothing demanded attention.
